body:
> Finally, i do wonder if we should be preferring .ts over .js in this case. I understand why that is the typescript semantics (they need types), but we don't, and if our assumption is simply that this .js file is the stripped .ts file then we should prefer it.

The intent of this feature is:
1. you are using Node.js native ESM. I.e. you need to use fully-specificed import specifiers with extensions
2. Typescript refuses to rewrite specifiers at all. Thus you **need** to include `.js` in the import specifier
3. You are writing Typescript files. Thus if you have a `./foo.js` that should still resolve to `foo.ts`

You can use use `tsc --traceResolution` to see what tsc does in this case, btw
author: mischnic

body: i agree it should resolve to foo.ts if that is the only thing that exists, but if both foo.js and foo.ts exist then resolving to foo.js makes more sense to me (this is also what node would do).  In the case that foo.js does not exist, yes we should resolve to foo.ts.  So im really just questioning the issue of priority
author: lukesandberg

body: ok, fixed a bug in the node-file-tracing code.  i still suspect that the assert in `EsmAssetReference` is not correct, but am looking to see what the integration tests say

body:
I think we might want to keep that.

We want to be able to alias resolved requests too:

```
resolveAlias: {
  [require.resolve("some/file")]: require.resolve("some/other-file"),
  [require.resolve("some/broken-file")]: false
}
```

At least that's possible in webpack, but we don't support that in Turbopack yet.
author: sokra
ijjk

author: mischnic

body: Why do you disable it?
author: eps1lon

body:
In this particular case, the question was: "why does `next dev` with Turbopack execute `nextConfig.generateBuildId`?"
This patching ignore-lists the next.js internals and obscured where that was getting called.

So the ignore listing is main problem when I try to find problems in Next.js internals
author: mischnic
